"[Anthem of the Canaries](/wiki/Anthem_of_the_Canaries "Anthem of the Canaries")" | |

([/kəˈnɛəri/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-canary.ogg "File:En-us-canary.ogg"); [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Canarias* [[kaˈnaɾjas]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Pronunciation_of_Canarias_in_Spanish.ogg "File:Pronunciation of Canarias in Spanish.ogg")) or **Canaries** are an [archipelago](/wiki/Archipelago "Archipelago") in the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") and the southernmost [autonomous community](/wiki/Autonomous_communities_of_Spain "Autonomous communities of Spain") of [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"), located about 100 kilometres (60 mi) off the northwest coast of [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"). The archipelago has a population of approximately 2.27 million inhabitants, making it the most populous overseas [special territory of the European Union](/wiki/Special_member_state_territories_and_the_European_Union "Special member state territories and the European Union").[[8]](#cite_note-Utreta-8)[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The **Canary Islands** ([/kəˈnɛəri/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-canary.ogg "File:En-us-canary.ogg"); [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Canarias* [[kaˈnaɾjas]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Pronunciation_of_Canarias_in_Spanish.ogg "File:Pronunciation of Canarias in Spanish.ogg")) or **Canaries** are an [archipelago](/wiki/Archipelago "Archipelago") in the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") and the southernmost [autonomous community](/wiki/Autonomous_communities_of_Spain "Autonomous communities of Spain") of [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"), located about 100 kilometres (60 mi) off the northwest coast of [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"). The archipelago has a population of approximately 2.27 million inhabitants, making it the most populous overseas [special territory of the European Union](/wiki/Special_member_state_territories_and_the_European_Union "Special member state territories and the European Union").[[8]](#cite_note-Utreta-8)[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The seven main islands, listed from largest to smallest by area, are [Tenerife](/wiki/Tenerife "Tenerife"), [Fuerteventura](/wiki/Fuerteventura "Fuerteventura"), [Gran Canaria](/wiki/Gran_Canaria "Gran Canaria"), [Lanzarote](/wiki/Lanzarote "Lanzarote"), [La Palma](/wiki/La_Palma "La Palma"), [La Gomera](/wiki/La_Gomera "La Gomera"), and [El Hierro](/wiki/El_Hierro "El Hierro"). The only other inhabited island is [La Graciosa](/wiki/Graciosa,_Canary_Islands "Graciosa, Canary Islands"), which is administratively dependent on Lanzarote. The archipelago also includes numerous smaller islands and islets, such as [Alegranza](/wiki/Alegranza "Alegranza"), [Isla de Lobos](/wiki/Islote_de_Lobos "Islote de Lobos"), [Montaña Clara](/wiki/Monta%C3%B1a_Clara "Montaña Clara"), [Roque del Oeste](/wiki/Roque_del_Oeste "Roque del Oeste"), and [Roque del Este](/wiki/Roque_del_Este "Roque del Este"), as well as various rocks, including [Garachico](/wiki/Roque_de_Garachico "Roque de Garachico") and [Anaga](/wiki/Roques_de_Anaga "Roques de Anaga"). Historically, the island chain was referred to as "the Fortunate Isles".[[11]](#cite_note-11) The Canary Islands are the southernmost and westernmost region of Spain as well as the largest and most populous archipelago of [Macaronesia](/wiki/Macaronesia "Macaronesia").[[12]](#cite_note-catalogo.museosdetenerife.org-12) They are also the largest and most populated archipelago in Spain. Owing to their strategic location, the Canary Islands have long been considered a link between [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"), [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), and the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In October 2025, the Canary Islands had a population of 2,268,035,[[14]](#cite_note-14) with a density of 305 inhabitants per km2, making them the seventh most populous autonomous community in Spain and the third-highest in population density, behind only [Madrid](/wiki/Community_of_Madrid "Community of Madrid") and the [Basque Country](/wiki/Basque_Country_(autonomous_community) "Basque Country (autonomous community)"). The population is mostly concentrated on the two capital islands, with around 43% on Tenerife and 39% on Gran Canaria. In 2026, the Canary Islands are expected to have a nominal GDP of €64.123 billion, resulting in a GDP per capita of €28,272.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

The Canary Islands, especially Tenerife, Gran Canaria, Fuerteventura, and Lanzarote, are a major tourist destination, with over 14.1 million visitors in 2023.[[16]](#cite_note-16) This is due to their beaches, [subtropical climate](/wiki/Subtropical_climate "Subtropical climate"), and important natural attractions, especially [Maspalomas](/wiki/Maspalomas "Maspalomas") in [Gran Canaria](/wiki/Gran_Canaria "Gran Canaria") and Mount [Teide](/wiki/Teide "Teide"), a [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") in [Tenerife](/wiki/Tenerife "Tenerife"). Mount Teide is the [highest peak in Spain](/wiki/List_of_mountains_in_Spain "List of mountains in Spain") and the [3rd tallest volcano in the world](/wiki/List_of_volcanoes_by_elevation "List of volcanoes by elevation"), measured from its base on the ocean floor.[[17]](#cite_note-gobiernodecanarias1-17) The islands have warm summers and winters warm enough for the climate to be technically tropical at sea level.[[18]](#cite_note-18) The amount of precipitation and the level of maritime moderation vary depending on location and elevation. The archipelago includes green areas as well as semi-desert. The islands' high mountains are ideal for astronomical observation, because they lie above the [temperature inversion](/wiki/Inversion_(meteorology) "Inversion (meteorology)") layer. As a result, the archipelago has two professional astronomical observatories: the [Teide Observatory](/wiki/Teide_Observatory "Teide Observatory") on Tenerife, and [Roque de los Muchachos Observatory](/wiki/Roque_de_los_Muchachos_Observatory "Roque de los Muchachos Observatory") on La Palma.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

In 1927, the [Province of Canary Islands](/wiki/Province_of_Canary_Islands "Province of Canary Islands") was split into two provinces, [Santa Cruz de Tenerife](/wiki/Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife_(province) "Santa Cruz de Tenerife (province)") and [Las Palmas](/wiki/Province_of_Las_Palmas "Province of Las Palmas"). In 1982, the autonomous community of the Canary Islands was established. The cities of [Santa Cruz de Tenerife](/wiki/Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife "Santa Cruz de Tenerife") and [Las Palmas de Gran Canaria](/wiki/Las_Palmas_de_Gran_Canaria "Las Palmas de Gran Canaria") are, jointly, the capitals of the islands.[[20]](#cite_note-estatuto2018-20)[[21]](#cite_note-es.wikisource.org-21)[[22]](#cite_note-gobiernodecanarias.org-22) Those cities are also, respectively, the capitals of the [provinces](/wiki/Spanish_provinces "Spanish provinces") of [Santa Cruz de Tenerife](/wiki/Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife_(province) "Santa Cruz de Tenerife (province)") and [Las Palmas](/wiki/Province_of_Las_Palmas "Province of Las Palmas"). Las Palmas de Gran Canaria has been the largest city in the Canaries since 1768, except for a brief period in the 1910s.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Between the [1833 territorial division of Spain](/wiki/1833_territorial_division_of_Spain "1833 territorial division of Spain") and 1927, Santa Cruz de Tenerife was the sole capital of the Canary Islands. In 1927, it was ordered by decree that the capital of the Canary Islands would be shared between two cities, and this arrangement persists to the present day.[[21]](#cite_note-es.wikisource.org-21)[[24]](#cite_note-Real_Decreto_Canarias-24) The third largest city in the Canary Islands is [San Cristóbal de La Laguna](/wiki/San_Crist%C3%B3bal_de_La_Laguna "San Cristóbal de La Laguna"), another World Heritage Site on Tenerife.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)

During the [Age of Sail](/wiki/Age_of_Sail "Age of Sail"), the islands were the main stopover for [Spanish galleons](/wiki/Spanish_treasure_fleet "Spanish treasure fleet") during the [Spanish colonisation of the Americas](/wiki/Spanish_colonisation_of_the_Americas "Spanish colonisation of the Americas"), which sailed that far south in order to catch the prevailing northeasterly [trade winds](/wiki/Trade_winds "Trade winds").[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canary_Islands&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The name *Islas Canarias* is likely derived from the Latin name *Canariae Insulae*, meaning 'Islands of the Dogs', perhaps because [monk seals](/wiki/Monk_seals "Monk seals") or sea dogs were abundant, a name that was evidently generalized from the ancient name of one of these islands, *Canaria* – presumably Gran Canaria. According to the historian [Pliny the Elder](/wiki/Pliny_the_Elder "Pliny the Elder"), the island *Canaria* contained "vast multitudes of dogs of very large size".[[30]](#cite_note-30) The connection to dogs is retained in their depiction on the islands' coat-of-arms.

Other theories speculate that the name comes from the [Nukkari](/wiki/Nukkari "Nukkari") Berber tribe living in the Moroccan Atlas, named in Roman sources as *Canarii*, though Pliny again mentions the relation of this term with dogs.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

The name of the islands is not derived from the [canary bird](/wiki/Atlantic_canary "Atlantic canary"); rather, the birds are named after the islands.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

[Canary Wharf](/wiki/Canary_Wharf "Canary Wharf"), located on the [Isle of Dogs](/wiki/Isle_of_Dogs "Isle of Dogs") in London, was named after a warehouse which handled imports from the islands.[[33]](#cite_note-Brit-33)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canary_Islands&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

### Ancient and pre-Hispanic times

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canary_Islands&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Ancient and pre-Hispanic times")]

Main article: [Canary Islands in pre-colonial times](/wiki/Canary_Islands_in_pre-colonial_times "Canary Islands in pre-colonial times")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/MNH_-_Mumie_Frau_1.jpg/250px-MNH_-_Mumie_Frau_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:MNH_-_Mumie_Frau_1.jpg)

[Guanche mummy](/wiki/Guanche_mummies "Guanche mummies") of a woman (830 AD). [Museo de la Naturaleza y el Hombre](/wiki/Museo_de_la_Naturaleza_y_el_Hombre "Museo de la Naturaleza y el Hombre"), [Santa Cruz de Tenerife](/wiki/Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife "Santa Cruz de Tenerife").

Before the arrival of humans, the Canaries were inhabited by prehistoric animals including the giant lizard (*[Gallotia goliath](/wiki/Gallotia_goliath "Gallotia goliath")*), the [Tenerife](/wiki/Tenerife_giant_rat "Tenerife giant rat") and [Gran Canaria giant rats](/wiki/Gran_Canaria_giant_rat "Gran Canaria giant rat"),[[34]](#cite_note-34) and giant tortoises, *[Geochelone burchardi](/wiki/Geochelone_burchardi "Geochelone burchardi")* and *[Geochelone vulcanica](/wiki/Geochelone_vulcanica "Geochelone vulcanica")*.

Although the original settlement of what are now called the Canary Islands is not entirely clear, linguistic, genetic, and archaeological analyses indicate that [indigenous](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples "Indigenous peoples") peoples were living on the Canary Islands at least 2,000 years ago, possibly 3,000, and that they shared a common origin with the [Amazighs](/wiki/Amazighs "Amazighs") on the nearby North African coast.[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-:3-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) Reaching the islands may have taken place using several small boats, landing on the easternmost islands Lanzarote and Fuerteventura. These groups came to be known collectively as the [Guanches](/wiki/Guanches "Guanches"), although *Guanches* had been the name for only the indigenous inhabitants of Tenerife.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

According to a 2024 study by the University of Las Palmas de Gran Canaria, there is archaeological evidence that the Romans were the first to establish contact with the islands, during the period from the 1st century [BCE](/wiki/Common_Era "Common Era") to the 1st century CE. There was no overlap with the occupation by the people who were inhabiting the islands at the time of the Spanish conquest, who had first arrived sometime between the 1st and 3rd centuries CE.[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/LomodelosGatos.jpg/250px-LomodelosGatos.jpg)](/wiki/File:LomodelosGatos.jpg)

A selection of artefacts unearthed from the Lomo de los Gatos site on Gran Canaria

As José Farrujia describes, "The indigenous Canarians lived mainly in natural caves, usually near the coast, 300 to 500 metres (1,000 to 1,600 ft) above sea level. These caves were sometimes isolated but more commonly formed settlements, with burial caves nearby".[[41]](#cite_note-:4-41) Archaeological work has uncovered a rich culture visible through artefacts of [ceramics](/wiki/Ceramic "Ceramic"), human figures, fishing, hunting and farming tools, plant fibre clothing and vessels, as well as cave paintings. At Lomo de los Gatos on Gran Canaria, a site occupied from 1,600 years ago up until the 1960s, round stone houses, complex burial sites, and associated artefacts have been found.[[42]](#cite_note-42) Across the islands are thousands of [Libyco-Berber](/wiki/Libyco-Berber "Libyco-Berber") alphabet inscriptions that have been extensively documented by many linguists.[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)

The social structure of indigenous Canarians encompassed "a system of [matrilineal](/wiki/Matrilineality "Matrilineality") descent in most of the islands, in which inheritance was passed on via the female line. Social status and wealth were hereditary and determined the individual's position in the social pyramid, which consisted of the king, the relatives of the king, the lower nobility, villeins, plebeians, and finally executioners, butchers, embalmers, and prisoners". Their religion was [animist](/wiki/Animism "Animism"), centring on the sun and moon, as well as natural features such as mountains.[[41]](#cite_note-:4-41)

### Exploration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canary_Islands&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Exploration")]

The islands may have been visited by the [Phoenicians](/wiki/Phoenicia "Phoenicia"), the [Greeks](/wiki/Greeks "Greeks"), and the [Carthaginians](/wiki/Ancient_Carthage "Ancient Carthage"). King [Juba II](/wiki/Juba_II "Juba II"), Caesar [Augustus](/wiki/Augustus "Augustus")'s [Numidian](/wiki/Numidia "Numidia") protégé, is credited with discovering the islands for the Western world. According to [Pliny the Elder](/wiki/Pliny_the_Elder "Pliny the Elder"), Juba found the islands uninhabited, but found "a small temple of stone" and "some traces of buildings".[[45]](#cite_note-Pliny_the_Elder_(77-79_AD)-45) Juba dispatched a naval contingent to re-open the dye production facility at [Mogador](/wiki/Essaouira "Essaouira") in what is now western Morocco in the early first century [AD](/wiki/AD "AD").[[46]](#cite_note-46) That same naval force was subsequently sent on an exploration of the Canary Islands, using Mogador as their mission base.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The names given by Romans to the individual islands were *Ninguaria* or *Nivaria* (Tenerife), *Canaria* (Gran Canaria), *Pluvialia* or *Invale* (Lanzarote), *Ombrion* (La Palma), *Planasia* (Fuerteventura), *Iunonia* or *Junonia* (El Hierro) and *Capraria* (La Gomera).[[47]](#cite_note-47)

From the 14th century onward, numerous visits were made by sailors from [Mallorca](/wiki/Mallorca "Mallorca"), [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal"), and [Genoa](/wiki/Genoa "Genoa"). [Lancelotto Malocello](/wiki/Lancelotto_Malocello "Lancelotto Malocello") settled on Lanzarote in 1312. The Mallorcans established a [mission](/wiki/Missionary "Missionary") with a bishop in the islands that lasted from 1350 to 1400.

### Castilian conquest

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canary_Islands&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Castilian conquest")]

Main articles: [Conquest of the Canary Islands](/wiki/Conquest_of_the_Canary_Islands "Conquest of the Canary Islands"), [Kingdom of the Canary Islands](/wiki/Kingdom_of_the_Canary_Islands "Kingdom of the Canary Islands"), [Crown of Castile](/wiki/Crown_of_Castile "Crown of Castile"), and [Kingdom of Portugal](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Portugal "Kingdom of Portugal")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Pueblochico_guanches_A.jpg/250px-Pueblochico_guanches_A.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pueblochico_guanches_A.jpg)

Reconstruction of a [Guanche](/wiki/Guanches "Guanches") settlement of Tenerife

In 1402, the Castilian colonisation of the islands began with the expedition of the French explorers [Jean de Béthencourt](/wiki/Jean_de_B%C3%A9thencourt "Jean de Béthencourt") and [Gadifer de la Salle](/wiki/Gadifer_de_la_Salle "Gadifer de la Salle"), nobles and [vassals](/wiki/Vassal "Vassal") of [Henry III of Castile](/wiki/Henry_III_of_Castile "Henry III of Castile"), to Lanzarote. From there, they went on to conquer Fuerteventura (1405) and El Hierro. These invasions were "brutal cultural and military clashes between the indigenous population and the Castilians" lasting over a century due to formidable resistance by indigenous Canarians.[[36]](#cite_note-:3-36) A professor of history has branded the conquest of the islands as a [genocide](/wiki/Genocide "Genocide") of the [Guanches](/wiki/Guanches "Guanches").[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49)

Béthencourt received the title King of the Canary Islands, but still recognised King Henry III as his overlord. It was not a simple military enterprise, given the aboriginal resistance on some islands. Neither was it politically, since the particular interests of the nobility (determined to strengthen their economic and political power through the acquisition of the islands) conflicted with those of the states, particularly Castile, which were in the midst of territorial expansion and in a process of strengthening of the crown against the nobility.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/AlonsoFernandezdeLugo2.JPG/330px-AlonsoFernandezdeLugo2.JPG)](/wiki/File:AlonsoFernandezdeLugo2.JPG)

[Alonso Fernández de Lugo](/wiki/Alonso_Fern%C3%A1ndez_de_Lugo "Alonso Fernández de Lugo") presenting the captured native Guanche kings of [Tenerife](/wiki/Tenerife "Tenerife") to the [Catholic Monarchs](/wiki/Catholic_Monarchs "Catholic Monarchs")

Historians distinguish two periods in the conquest of the Canary Islands:

**Aristocratic conquest** (*conquista señorial*): This refers to the early conquests carried out by the nobility, for their own benefit and without the direct participation of the Crown of Castile, which merely granted rights of conquest in exchange for pacts of vassalage between the noble conqueror and the Crown. One can identify within this period an early phase known as the Betancurian or Norman conquest, carried out by [Jean de Bethencourt](/wiki/Jean_de_Bethencourt "Jean de Bethencourt") (who was originally from Normandy) and [Gadifer de la Salle](/wiki/Gadifer_de_la_Salle "Gadifer de la Salle") between 1402 and 1405, which involved the islands of [Lanzarote](/wiki/Lanzarote "Lanzarote"), [El Hierro](/wiki/El_Hierro "El Hierro"), and [Fuerteventura](/wiki/Fuerteventura "Fuerteventura"). The subsequent phase is known as the Castilian conquest, carried out by Castilian nobles who acquired, through purchases, assignments and marriages, the previously conquered islands and also incorporated the island of [La Gomera](/wiki/La_Gomera "La Gomera") around 1450.

**Royal conquest** (*conquista realenga*): This defines the conquest between 1478 and 1496, carried out directly by the Crown of Castile, during the reign of the [Catholic Monarchs](/wiki/Catholic_Monarchs "Catholic Monarchs"), who armed and partly financed the conquest of those islands which were still unconquered: [Gran Canaria](/wiki/Gran_Canaria "Gran Canaria"), [La Palma](/wiki/La_Palma "La Palma") and [Tenerife](/wiki/Tenerife "Tenerife"). This phase of the conquest came to an end in the year 1496, with the dominion of the island of Tenerife, bringing the entire Canarian Archipelago under the control of the Crown of Castile.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Columbus_House-Vegueta-Las_Palmas_Gran_Canaria.jpg/250px-Columbus_House-Vegueta-Las_Palmas_Gran_Canaria.jpg)](/wiki/File:Columbus_House-Vegueta-Las_Palmas_Gran_Canaria.jpg)

Casa de Colón (Las Palmas de Gran Canaria), which Christopher Columbus visited during his first trip

Béthencourt also established a base on the island of La Gomera, but it would be many years before the island was fully conquered. The natives of La Gomera, and of Gran Canaria, Tenerife, and La Palma, resisted the Castilian invaders for almost a century. In 1448, Maciot de Béthencourt sold the lordship of Lanzarote to Portugal's Prince [Henry the Navigator](/wiki/Henry_the_Navigator "Henry the Navigator"), an action that was accepted by neither the natives nor the Castilians. Despite [Pope Nicholas V](/wiki/Pope_Nicholas_V#cite_ref-8 "Pope Nicholas V") ruling that the Canary Islands were under Portuguese control, the crisis swelled to a revolt which lasted until 1459 with the final expulsion of the Portuguese. In 1479, Portugal and Castile signed the [Treaty of Alcáçovas](/wiki/Treaty_of_Alc%C3%A1%C3%A7ovas "Treaty of Alcáçovas"), which settled disputes between Castile and Portugal over the control of the Atlantic. This treaty recognized Castilian control of the Canary Islands but also confirmed Portuguese possession of the [Azores](/wiki/Azores "Azores"), [Madeira](/wiki/Madeira "Madeira"), and the [Cape Verde islands](/wiki/Cape_Verde "Cape Verde"), and gave the Portuguese rights to any further islands or lands in the Atlantic that might be discovered.

The Castilians continued to dominate the islands, but due to the topography and the resistance of the native Guanches, they did not achieve complete control until 1496, when Tenerife and La Palma were finally subdued by [Alonso Fernández de Lugo](/wiki/Alonso_Fern%C3%A1ndez_de_Lugo "Alonso Fernández de Lugo"). As a result of this "the native pre-Hispanic population declined quickly due to war, epidemics, and slavery".[[51]](#cite_note-:5-51) The Canaries were incorporated into the [Kingdom of Castile](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Castile "Kingdom of Castile").

### After the conquest and the introduction of slavery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canary_Islands&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: After the conquest and the introduction of slavery")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Canary_Islands_map_by_William_Dampier_1699_-_Project_Gutenberg_eText_15675.jpg/250px-Canary_Islands_map_by_William_Dampier_1699_-_Project_Gutenberg_eText_15675.jpg)](/wiki/File:Canary_Islands_map_by_William_Dampier_1699_-_Project_Gutenberg_eText_15675.jpg)

Maps of the Canary Islands drawn by [William Dampier](/wiki/William_Dampier "William Dampier") during his voyage to [New Holland](/wiki/Dutch_West_India_Company "Dutch West India Company") in 1699

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Coat_of_Arms_of_the_Realm_of_Canary_Islands.svg/250px-Coat_of_Arms_of_the_Realm_of_Canary_Islands.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Coat_of_Arms_of_the_Realm_of_Canary_Islands.svg)

Coat of arms of the Castilian and Spanish Realm of Canary Islands

After the conquest, the Castilians imposed a new economic model, based on single-crop cultivation: first [sugarcane](/wiki/Sugarcane "Sugarcane"); then wine, an important item of trade with [England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England"). Gran Canaria was conquered by the [Crown of Castile](/wiki/Crown_of_Castile "Crown of Castile") on 6 March 1480, and Tenerife was conquered in 1496, and each had its own governor. There has been speculation that the abundance of *[Roccella tinctoria](/wiki/Roccella_tinctoria "Roccella tinctoria")* on the Canary Islands offered a profit motive for [Jean de Béthencourt](/wiki/Jean_de_B%C3%A9thencourt "Jean de Béthencourt") during his conquest of the islands. Lichen has been used for centuries to make dyes. This includes royal purple colors derived from *R. tinctoria*, also known as orseille.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Bimbaches.jpg/250px-Bimbaches.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bimbaches.jpg)

Painting of [Bimbache](/wiki/Bimbache "Bimbache") of [El Hierro](/wiki/El_Hierro "El Hierro") by Leonardo Torriani, 1592

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Charles_Edward_Dixon_HMS_St_George_1662_Battle_of_Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife_1657_Admiral_Robert_Blake.jpg/250px-Charles_Edward_Dixon_HMS_St_George_1662_Battle_of_Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife_1657_Admiral_Robert_Blake.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charles_Edward_Dixon_HMS_St_George_1662_Battle_of_Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife_1657_Admiral_Robert_Blake.jpg)

Robert Blake's flagship at the [Battle of Santa Cruz de Tenerife](/wiki/Battle_of_Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife_(1657) "Battle of Santa Cruz de Tenerife (1657)") during the [Anglo-Spanish War](/wiki/Anglo-Spanish_War_(1654%E2%80%931660) "Anglo-Spanish War (1654–1660)"), 1657

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Slave-driving_%28Africa%2C_1878%2C_p._307%29.jpg/250px-Slave-driving_%28Africa%2C_1878%2C_p._307%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Slave-driving_(Africa,_1878,_p._307).jpg)

[Slave-driving in the interior of Africa](/wiki/Slavery_in_Africa "Slavery in Africa") in order to sell into enforced labour

The objective of the Spanish Crown to convert the islands into a powerhouse of cultivation required a much larger labour force.[[53]](#cite_note-Pérez-53) This was attained through a practice of enslavement, not only of indigenous Canarians, but large numbers of Africans who were taken from North and Sub-Saharan Africa.[[54]](#cite_note-54) Whilst the first slave plantations in the Atlantic region were across [Madeira](/wiki/Madeira "Madeira"), [Cape Verde](/wiki/Cape_Verde "Cape Verde"), and the Canary Islands, it was only the Canary Islands which had an indigenous population and were therefore invaded rather than newly occupied.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

Because this agriculture industry was largely based on sugarcane, the Castilians converted large swaths of the landscape for sugarcane production and the processing and manufacturing of sugar, largely enabled by slave labour.[[56]](#cite_note-56) The cities of [Santa Cruz de Tenerife](/wiki/Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife "Santa Cruz de Tenerife") and [Las Palmas de Gran Canaria](/wiki/Las_Palmas_de_Gran_Canaria "Las Palmas de Gran Canaria") became a stopping point for the Spanish traders, as well as [conquistadors](/wiki/Conquistador "Conquistador"), and missionaries on their way to the [New World](/wiki/New_World "New World"). This trade route brought great wealth to the Castilian social sectors of the islands and soon were attracting merchants and adventurers from all over Europe. As wealth increased, enslaved Africans were also forced into more domestic roles for the richer Castilians on the islands.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

Research on the skeletons of some of the enslaved from the burial site of Finca Clavijo on Gran Canaria has shown that "all of the adults buried in Finca Clavijo undertook extensive physical activity that involved significant stress on the spine and appendicular skeleton", a result of relentless hard labour, akin to the physical abnormalities found within other enslaved groups from sugarcane plantations around the world.[[51]](#cite_note-:5-51)

As a result of the huge wealth generated[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*], magnificent palaces and churches were built on La Palma during this busy, prosperous period. The Church of El Salvador survives as one of the island's finest examples of the architecture of the 16th century. Civilian architecture survives in forms such as [Casas de los Sánchez-Ochando](/wiki/Casas_de_los_S%C3%A1nchez-Ochando "Casas de los Sánchez-Ochando") or [Casa Quintana](/wiki/Casa_Quintana "Casa Quintana").

The Canaries' wealth invited attacks by [pirates](/wiki/Pirate "Pirate") and [privateers](/wiki/Privateer "Privateer"). [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_people "Turkish people") [admiral](/wiki/Admiral "Admiral") and privateer [Kemal Reis](/wiki/Kemal_Reis "Kemal Reis") ventured into the Canaries in 1501, while [Murat Reis the Elder](/wiki/Murat_Reis_the_Elder "Murat Reis the Elder") captured Lanzarote in 1585.

The most severe attack took place in 1599, during the [Dutch Revolt](/wiki/Dutch_Revolt "Dutch Revolt"). A [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_people "Dutch people") fleet of 74 ships and 12,000 men, commanded by [Pieter van der Does](/wiki/Pieter_van_der_Does "Pieter van der Does"), attacked the capital Las Palmas de Gran Canaria (the city had 3,500 of Gran Canaria's 8,545 inhabitants). The Dutch attacked the Castillo de la Luz, which guarded the harbor. The Canarians evacuated civilians from the city, and the Castillo surrendered (but not the city). The Dutch moved inland, but Canarian cavalry drove them back to Tamaraceite, near the city.

The Dutch then laid siege to the city, demanding the surrender of all its wealth. They received 12 sheep and 3 calves. Furious, the Dutch sent 4,000 soldiers to attack the Council of the Canaries, who were sheltering in the village of Santa Brígida. Three hundred Canarian soldiers ambushed the Dutch in the village of Monte Lentiscal, killing 150 and forcing the rest to retreat. The Dutch concentrated on Las Palmas de Gran Canaria, attempting to burn it down. The Dutch pillaged [Maspalomas](/wiki/Maspalomas "Maspalomas"), on the southern coast of Gran Canaria, [San Sebastián](/wiki/San_Sebasti%C3%A1n_de_La_Gomera "San Sebastián de La Gomera") on La Gomera, and [Santa Cruz](/wiki/Santa_Cruz_de_La_Palma "Santa Cruz de La Palma") on La Palma, but eventually gave up the siege of Las Palmas and withdrew.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Christelijke_gevangenen_worden_op_een_plein_te_Algiers_als_slaaf_verkocht%2C_Jan_Luyken%2C_1684.jpg/250px-Christelijke_gevangenen_worden_op_een_plein_te_Algiers_als_slaaf_verkocht%2C_Jan_Luyken%2C_1684.jpg)](/wiki/File:Christelijke_gevangenen_worden_op_een_plein_te_Algiers_als_slaaf_verkocht,_Jan_Luyken,_1684.jpg)

Christian prisoners are [sold as slaves](/wiki/Barbary_slave_trade "Barbary slave trade") in a square in Algiers. [Barbary pirates](/wiki/Barbary_pirates "Barbary pirates") captured almost 2,000 Canarians during four invasions between 1569 and 1618.[[53]](#cite_note-Pérez-53)

In 1618 the [Barbary pirates](/wiki/Barbary_pirates "Barbary pirates") from North Africa attacked Lanzarote and La Gomera taking 1000 captives to be sold as [slaves](/wiki/Barbary_slave_trade "Barbary slave trade").[[58]](#cite_note-58) Another noteworthy attack occurred in 1797, when [Santa Cruz de Tenerife](/wiki/Battle_of_Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife_(1797) "Battle of Santa Cruz de Tenerife (1797)") was attacked by a British fleet under [Horatio Nelson](/wiki/Horatio_Nelson,_1st_Viscount_Nelson "Horatio Nelson, 1st Viscount Nelson") on 25 July. The British were repulsed, losing almost 400 men. It was during this battle that Nelson lost his right arm.

Apart from the passage of [Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus"), the Canary Islands were the site of some of the most important fleets in Western history. Such as the fleet of the [Virginia Company](/wiki/Virginia_Company "Virginia Company") in 1606, which marked the foundation of [Fort Jamestown](/wiki/Jamestown,_Virginia "Jamestown, Virginia") -the first permanent English settlement in what is now the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States")-[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60) and the passage of the [First Fleet](/wiki/First_Fleet "First Fleet") through Tenerife, which marked the first European settlement in [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") in [Botany Bay](/wiki/Botany_Bay "Botany Bay").[[61]](#cite_note-61)

### 18th to 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canary_Islands&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 18th to 19th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Amaro_Pargo.jpg/250px-Amaro_Pargo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Amaro_Pargo.jpg)

[Amaro Pargo](/wiki/Amaro_Pargo "Amaro Pargo") (1678–1741), [corsair](/wiki/Piracy "Piracy") and [merchant](/wiki/Merchant "Merchant") from Tenerife who participated in the [Spanish treasure fleet](/wiki/Spanish_treasure_fleet "Spanish treasure fleet"), the Spanish-American trade route

The sugar-based economy of the islands faced stiff competition from Spain's [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean") colonies. Low sugar prices in the 19th century caused severe recessions on the islands. A new cash crop, [cochineal](/wiki/Cochineal "Cochineal") (*cochinilla*), came into cultivation during this time, reinvigorating the islands' economy. During this time the Canarian-American trade was developed, in which Canarian products such as cochineal, sugarcane and rum were sold in American ports such as [Veracruz](/wiki/Veracruz "Veracruz"), [Campeche](/wiki/Campeche "Campeche"), [La Guaira](/wiki/La_Guaira "La Guaira") and [Havana](/wiki/Havana "Havana"), among others.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

By the end of the 18th century, Canary Islanders had already emigrated to Spanish American territories, such as [Havana](/wiki/Havana "Havana"), [Veracruz](/wiki/Veracruz "Veracruz"), and [Santo Domingo](/wiki/Santo_Domingo "Santo Domingo"),[[63]](#cite_note-63) [San Antonio, Texas](/wiki/San_Antonio,_Texas "San Antonio, Texas")[[64]](#cite_note-64) and [St. Bernard Parish, Louisiana](/wiki/St._Bernard_Parish,_Louisiana "St. Bernard Parish, Louisiana").[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-66) These economic difficulties spurred mass emigration during the 19th and first half of the 20th century, primarily to the Americas. Between 1840 and 1890 as many as 40,000 Canary Islanders emigrated to [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela "Venezuela"). Also, thousands of Canarians moved to [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico") where the Spanish monarchy felt that Canarians would adapt to island life better than other immigrants from the mainland of Spain. Deeply entrenched traditions, such as the Mascaras Festival in the town of [Hatillo, Puerto Rico](/wiki/Hatillo,_Puerto_Rico "Hatillo, Puerto Rico"), are an example of Canarian culture still preserved in Puerto Rico. Similarly, many thousands of Canarians emigrated to the shores of [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba").[[67]](#cite_note-67) During the [Spanish–American War](/wiki/Spanish%E2%80%93American_War "Spanish–American War") of 1898, the Spanish fortified the islands against a possible American attack, but no such event took place.

### Romantic period and scientific expeditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canary_Islands&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Romantic period and scientific expeditions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Alfred_Diston_-_Square_in_the_Village_of_Los_Silos%2C_Tenerife_-_B1986.29.83_-_Yale_Center_for_British_Art.jpg/250px-Alfred_Diston_-_Square_in_the_Village_of_Los_Silos%2C_Tenerife_-_B1986.29.83_-_Yale_Center_for_British_Art.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alfred_Diston_-_Square_in_the_Village_of_Los_Silos,_Tenerife_-_B1986.29.83_-_Yale_Center_for_British_Art.jpg)

Square in the village of [Los Silos](/wiki/Los_Silos "Los Silos"), Tenerife, by [Alfred Diston](/wiki/Alfred_Diston "Alfred Diston"), 1827

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Coast_El_Golfo.JPG/250px-Coast_El_Golfo.JPG)](/wiki/File:Coast_El_Golfo.JPG)

Coast El Golfo, El Hierro

Sirera and Renn (2004)[[68]](#cite_note-68) distinguish two different types of expeditions, or voyages, during the period 1770–1830, which they term "the Romantic period":

First are "expeditions financed by the States, closely related with the official scientific Institutions. characterised by having strict scientific objectives (and inspired by) the spirit of Illustration and progress". In this type of expedition, Sirera and Renn include the following travellers:

* J. Edens, whose 1715 ascent and observations of Mt. Teide influenced many subsequent expeditions.
* [Louis Feuillée](/wiki/Louis_Feuill%C3%A9e "Louis Feuillée") (1724), who was sent to measure the meridian of El Hierro and to map the islands.
* [Jean-Charles de Borda](/wiki/Jean-Charles_de_Borda "Jean-Charles de Borda") (1771, 1776) who more accurately measured the longitudes of the islands and the height of Mount Teide
* the [Baudin](/wiki/Nicolas_Baudin "Nicolas Baudin")-Ledru expedition (1796) which aimed to recover a valuable collection of natural history objects.

The second type of expedition identified by Sirera and Renn is one that took place starting from more or less private initiatives. Among these, the key exponents were the following:

* [Alexander von Humboldt](/wiki/Alexander_von_Humboldt "Alexander von Humboldt") (1799)
* [Buch](/wiki/Christian_Leopold_von_Buch "Christian Leopold von Buch") and [Smith](/wiki/Christen_Smith_(botanist) "Christen Smith (botanist)") (1815)
* [Broussonet](/wiki/Broussonet "Broussonet")
* [Webb](/wiki/Philip_Barker_Webb "Philip Barker Webb")
* [Sabin Berthelot](/wiki/Sabin_Berthelot "Sabin Berthelot").

Sirera and Renn identify the period 1770–1830 as one in which "In a panorama dominated until that moment by France and England enters with strength and brio Germany of the Romantic period whose presence in the islands will increase".

### Early 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canary_Islands&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Early 20th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Bahia_luz_las_palmas_1912_jordao_da_luz_perestello.jpg/250px-Bahia_luz_las_palmas_1912_jordao_da_luz_perestello.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bahia_luz_las_palmas_1912_jordao_da_luz_perestello.jpg)

The [port of Las Palmas](/wiki/Port_of_Las_Palmas "Port of Las Palmas") in 1912

At the beginning of the 20th century, the British introduced a new [cash-crop](/wiki/Cash-crop "Cash-crop"), the [banana](/wiki/Banana "Banana"), the export of which was controlled by companies such as [Fyffes](/wiki/Fyffes "Fyffes").

30 November 1833 the [Province of Canary Islands](/wiki/Province_of_Canary_Islands "Province of Canary Islands") had been created with the capital being declared as Santa Cruz de Tenerife.[[69]](#cite_note-69) The rivalry between the cities of Las Palmas de Gran Canaria and Santa Cruz de Tenerife for the capital of the islands led to the division of the [archipelago](/wiki/Archipelago "Archipelago") into two provinces on 23 September 1927.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

During the time of the [Second Spanish Republic](/wiki/Second_Spanish_Republic "Second Spanish Republic"), [Marxist](/wiki/Marxist "Marxist") and [anarchist](/wiki/Anarchist "Anarchist") workers' movements began to develop, led by figures such as Jose Miguel Perez and [Guillermo Ascanio](/wiki/Guillermo_Ascanio "Guillermo Ascanio"). However, outside of a few municipalities, these organisations were a minority and fell easily to Nationalist forces during the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War").

### Franco regime

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canary_Islands&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Franco regime")]

Main article: [Francoist Spain](/wiki/Francoist_Spain "Francoist Spain")

In 1936, [Francisco Franco](/wiki/Francisco_Franco "Francisco Franco") was appointed General Commandant of the Canaries. He joined the military revolt of 17 July which began the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War"). Franco quickly took control of the archipelago, except for a few points of resistance on La Palma and in the town of [Vallehermoso](/wiki/Vallehermoso,_Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife "Vallehermoso, Santa Cruz de Tenerife"), on La Gomera. Though there was never a war in the islands, the post-war suppression of political dissent on the Canaries was most severe.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

During the Second World War, [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill") prepared plans for the British seizure of the Canary Islands as a [naval base](/wiki/Naval_base "Naval base"), in the event of [Gibraltar](/wiki/Gibraltar "Gibraltar") being invaded from the Spanish mainland.[[72]](#cite_note-72)[[Note 1]](#cite_note-73) The planned operation was known as [Operation Pilgrim](/wiki/Operation_Pilgrim "Operation Pilgrim").[[73]](#cite_note-74)

Opposition to Franco's regime did not begin to organise until the late 1950s, which experienced an upheaval of parties such as the [Communist Party of Spain](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Spain_(main) "Communist Party of Spain (main)") and the formation of various nationalist, leftist parties.

During the [Ifni War](/wiki/Ifni_War "Ifni War"), the Franco regime set up [concentration camps](/wiki/Concentration_camps "Concentration camps") on the islands to [extrajudicially imprison](/wiki/Extrajudicial_punishment "Extrajudicial punishment") those in Western Sahara suspected of disloyalty to Spain, many of whom were colonial troops recruited on the spot but were later deemed to be potential [fifth columnists](/wiki/Fifth_column "Fifth column") and deported to the Canary Islands. These camps were characterised by the use of forced labour for infrastructure projects and highly unsanitary conditions resulting in the widespread occurrence of [tuberculosis](/wiki/Tuberculosis "Tuberculosis").[[74]](#cite_note-75)

[Auditorio de Tenerife](/wiki/Auditorio_de_Tenerife "Auditorio de Tenerife") by [Santiago Calatrava](/wiki/Santiago_Calatrava "Santiago Calatrava"), and an icon of contemporary architecture in the Canary Islands, ([Santa Cruz de Tenerife](/wiki/Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife "Santa Cruz de Tenerife"))

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/EU_OCT_and_OMR_map_en.png/500px-EU_OCT_and_OMR_map_en.png)](/wiki/File:EU_OCT_and_OMR_map_en.png)

After the death of Franco, there was a pro-independence armed movement based in [Algeria](/wiki/Algeria "Algeria"), the [Movement for the Independence and Self-determination of the Canaries Archipelago](/wiki/Canary_Islands_Independence_Movement "Canary Islands Independence Movement") (MAIAC). In 1968, the [Organisation of African Unity](/wiki/Organisation_of_African_Unity "Organisation of African Unity") recognized the MAIAC as a legitimate [African independence movement](/wiki/African_independence_movements "African independence movements"), and declared the Canary Islands as an African territory still under foreign rule.[[75]](#cite_note-76)

## Islands

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canary_Islands&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Islands")]

From west to east, the Canary Islands are [El Hierro](/wiki/El_Hierro "El Hierro"), [La Palma](/wiki/La_Palma "La Palma"), [La Gomera](/wiki/La_Gomera "La Gomera"), [Tenerife](/wiki/Tenerife "Tenerife"), [Gran Canaria](/wiki/Gran_Canaria "Gran Canaria"), [Fuerteventura](/wiki/Fuerteventura "Fuerteventura"), [Lanzarote](/wiki/Lanzarote "Lanzarote"), and [La Graciosa](/wiki/Graciosa,_Canary_Islands "Graciosa, Canary Islands"). North of Lanzarote are the islets of [Montaña Clara](/wiki/Monta%C3%B1a_Clara "Montaña Clara"), [Alegranza](/wiki/Alegranza "Alegranza"), [Roque del Este](/wiki/Roque_del_Este "Roque del Este") and [Roque del Oeste](/wiki/Roque_del_Oeste "Roque del Oeste"), belonging to the [Chinijo Archipelago](/wiki/Chinijo_Archipelago "Chinijo Archipelago"). Northeast of Fuerteventura is the islet of [Lobos](/wiki/Lobos_Island "Lobos Island"). There are a series of small adjacent rocks in the Canary Islands: the [Roques de Anaga](/wiki/Roques_de_Anaga "Roques de Anaga"), [Garachico](/wiki/Roque_de_Garachico "Roque de Garachico") and Fasnia in Tenerife, and Salmor and Bonanza in El Hierro.

### El Hierro

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canary_Islands&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: El Hierro")]

Main article: [El Hierro](/wiki/El_Hierro "El Hierro")

El Hierro, the westernmost island, covers 268.71 km2 (103.75 sq mi). It is the second smallest of the major islands, and the least populous with 10,798 inhabitants. The whole island was declared a Reserve of the Biosphere in 2000. Its capital is [Valverde](/wiki/Valverde,_Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife "Valverde, Santa Cruz de Tenerife"). Also known as Ferro, it was once the westernmost known land in the world. Ancient European geographers such as Ptolemy recognised the island as the [prime meridian](/wiki/Prime_meridian "Prime meridian") of longitude with the [Ferro meridian](/wiki/Ferro_meridian "Ferro meridian"). That remained so until the 19th century, when it was displaced by the one [passing through Greenwich](/wiki/Greenwich_Mean_Time "Greenwich Mean Time").[[76]](#cite_note-77)[[77]](#cite_note-78)

### Fuerteventura

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canary_Islands&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Fuerteventura")]

Main article: [Fuerteventura](/wiki/Fuerteventura "Fuerteventura")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/View_from_Pico_de_la_Palma%2C_Fuerteventura.jpg/960px-View_from_Pico_de_la_Palma%2C_Fuerteventura.jpg)](/wiki/File:View_from_Pico_de_la_Palma,_Fuerteventura.jpg "View from Pico de la Palma, Fuerteventura")

View from Pico de la Palma, Fuerteventura

Fuerteventura, with a surface of 1,660 km2 (640 sq mi), is the second largest island of the archipelago. It has been declared a [biosphere reserve](/wiki/World_Network_of_Biosphere_Reserves "World Network of Biosphere Reserves") by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO"). It is the oldest of the islands being more eroded. Its highest point is the Pico de la Zarza, at a height of 807 metres (2,648 feet). Its capital is [Puerto del Rosario](/wiki/Puerto_del_Rosario "Puerto del Rosario").

### Gran Canaria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canary_Islands&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Gran Canaria")]

Main article: [Gran Canaria](/wiki/Gran_Canaria "Gran Canaria")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Pueblo_de_Fataga.jpg/250px-Pueblo_de_Fataga.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pueblo_de_Fataga.jpg)

View of [Fataga](/wiki/Fataga "Fataga"), Gran Canaria

Gran Canaria has 846,717 inhabitants. The capital, [Las Palmas de Gran Canaria](/wiki/Las_Palmas "Las Palmas"), with 377,203 inhabitants, is the most populous city and shares the status of capital of the Canaries with Santa Cruz de Tenerife. Gran Canaria's surface area is 1,560 km2 (600 sq mi). Roque Nublo 1,813 metres (5,948 feet) and Pico de las Nieves ("Peak of Snow") 1,949 metres (6,394 feet) are located in the center of the island. On the south of the island are the Maspalomas Dunes (Gran Canaria).

### La Gomera

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canary_Islands&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: La Gomera")]

Main article: [La Gomera](/wiki/La_Gomera "La Gomera")

La Gomera (informally known as 'Isla Colombina') has an area of 369.76 km2 (142.77 sq mi) and is the second least populous island with 21,136 inhabitants. It has been declared a [biosphere reserve](/wiki/World_Network_of_Biosphere_Reserves "World Network of Biosphere Reserves") by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO"). Geologically it is one of the oldest of the archipelago. The insular capital is [San Sebastian de La Gomera](/wiki/San_Sebastian_de_La_Gomera "San Sebastian de La Gomera"). [Garajonay National Park](/wiki/Garajonay_National_Park "Garajonay National Park") is located on the island.

### Lanzarote

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canary_Islands&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Lanzarote")]

Main article: [Lanzarote](/wiki/Lanzarote "Lanzarote")

Lanzarote is the easternmost island and one of the oldest of the archipelago, and it has shown evidence of recent volcanic activity. It has a surface of 845.94 km2 (326.62 sq mi), and a population of 149,183 inhabitants, including the adjacent islets of the Chinijo Archipelago. The capital is [Arrecife](/wiki/Arrecife "Arrecife"), with 56,834 inhabitants.

#### Chinijo Archipelago

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canary_Islands&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Chinijo Archipelago")]

Main article: [Chinijo Archipelago](/wiki/Chinijo_Archipelago "Chinijo Archipelago")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/The_Chinijo.jpg/250px-The_Chinijo.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Chinijo.jpg)

The Chinijo Archipelago, seen from Lanzarote

The Chinijo Archipelago includes the islands [La Graciosa](/wiki/La_Graciosa "La Graciosa"), [Alegranza](/wiki/Alegranza "Alegranza"), [Montaña Clara](/wiki/Monta%C3%B1a_Clara "Montaña Clara"), [Roque del Este](/wiki/Roque_del_Este "Roque del Este") and [Roque del Oeste](/wiki/Roque_del_Oeste "Roque del Oeste"). It has a surface of 40.8 km2 (15.8 sq mi), and only La Graciosa is populated, with 658 inhabitants. With 29 km2 (11 sq mi), La Graciosa is the largest island of the Chinijo Archipelago and the smallest inhabited island of the Canaries.

##### La Graciosa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canary_Islands&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: La Graciosa")]

Main article: [La Graciosa](/wiki/La_Graciosa "La Graciosa")

Graciosa Island or commonly La Graciosa is a volcanic island in the Canary Islands of Spain, located 2 km (1.2 mi) north of the island of Lanzarote across the Strait of El Río. It was formed by the Canary hotspot. The island is part of the Chinijo Archipelago and the Chinijo Archipelago Natural Park (Parque Natural del Archipiélago Chinijo). It is administered by the municipality of Teguise. In 2018, La Graciosa was declared as *the eighth Canary Island* by the [Spanish Senate](/wiki/Spanish_Senate "Spanish Senate"),[[78]](#cite_note-Monzón2019-79)[[79]](#cite_note-80)[[80]](#cite_note-81) though it is not recognized as such by the Canarian administration. It is administratively dependent on the island of Lanzarote. It is the smallest and least populated of the main islands, with about 700 people.

### La Palma

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canary_Islands&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: La Palma")]

Main article: [La Palma](/wiki/La_Palma "La Palma")

La Palma, with 81,863 inhabitants covering an area of 708.32 km2 (273.48 sq mi), is in its entirety a biosphere reserve. For long it showed no signs of volcanic activity, even though the volcano [Teneguía](/wiki/Tenegu%C3%ADa "Teneguía") entered into eruption last in 1971. On 19 September 2021, the volcanic [Cumbre Vieja](/wiki/Cumbre_Vieja "Cumbre Vieja") on the island [erupted](/wiki/2021_Cumbre_Vieja_volcanic_eruption "2021 Cumbre Vieja volcanic eruption").[[81]](#cite_note-82) It is the second-highest island of the Canaries, with the Roque de los Muchachos at 2,423 metres (7,949 feet) as its highest point. [Santa Cruz de La Palma](/wiki/Santa_Cruz_de_La_Palma "Santa Cruz de La Palma"), known to those on the island as simply "Santa Cruz", is its capital.

### Tenerife

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canary_Islands&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Tenerife")]

Main article: [Tenerife](/wiki/Tenerife "Tenerife")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Urville-Laguna.jpg/250px-Urville-Laguna.jpg)](/wiki/File:Urville-Laguna.jpg)

[San Cristóbal de La Laguna](/wiki/San_Crist%C3%B3bal_de_La_Laguna "San Cristóbal de La Laguna") in 1880 (Tenerife)

Tenerife is, with its area of 2,034 km2 (785 sq mi), the most extensive island of the Canary Islands. With 904,713 inhabitants, it is the most populated island of the archipelago and Spain. Two of the islands' principal cities are located on it: the capital, [Santa Cruz de Tenerife](/wiki/Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife "Santa Cruz de Tenerife") and [San Cristóbal de La Laguna](/wiki/San_Crist%C3%B3bal_de_La_Laguna "San Cristóbal de La Laguna"), a World Heritage Site. [San Cristóbal de La Laguna](/wiki/San_Crist%C3%B3bal_de_La_Laguna "San Cristóbal de La Laguna"), the second city of the island, is home to the oldest university in the Canary Islands, the [University of La Laguna](/wiki/University_of_La_Laguna "University of La Laguna"). [Teide](/wiki/Teide "Teide"), with its 3,715 metres (12,188 feet) is the highest peak of [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") and a [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"). [Tenerife](/wiki/Tenerife "Tenerife") is the site of the [worst air disaster](/wiki/Tenerife_airport_disaster "Tenerife airport disaster") in the history of aviation, in which 583 people were killed in the collision of two [Boeing 747s](/wiki/Boeing_747 "Boeing 747") on 27 March 1977.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canary_Islands&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Map_of_the_Canary_Islands.svg/500px-Map_of_the_Canary_Islands.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_the_Canary_Islands.svg)

A map of the Canary Islands

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Hacha_grande_from_papagayo_pano.jpg/500px-Hacha_grande_from_papagayo_pano.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hacha_grande_from_papagayo_pano.jpg)

[Hacha Grande](/wiki/Hacha_Grande "Hacha Grande"), a mountain in the south of Lanzarote, viewed from the road to the Playa de Papagayo

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Grancanaria.jpg/500px-Grancanaria.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grancanaria.jpg)

A panoramic view of Gran Canaria, with [Roque Nublo](/wiki/Roque_Nublo "Roque Nublo") at the left and [Roque Bentayga](/wiki/Roque_Bentayga "Roque Bentayga") at the center

[Tenerife](/wiki/Tenerife "Tenerife") is the largest and most populous island of the archipelago. [Gran Canaria](/wiki/Gran_Canaria "Gran Canaria"), with 865,070 inhabitants, is both the Canary Islands' second most populous island, and the third most populous one in Spain after Tenerife (966,354 inhabitants) and [Mallorca](/wiki/Mallorca "Mallorca") (896,038 inhabitants).[[83]](#cite_note-84) The island of [Fuerteventura](/wiki/Fuerteventura "Fuerteventura") is the second largest in the archipelago and located 100 km (62 mi) from the African coast.

The islands form the [Macaronesia](/wiki/Macaronesia "Macaronesia") [ecoregion](/wiki/Ecoregion "Ecoregion") with the [Azores](/wiki/Azores "Azores"), [Cape Verde](/wiki/Cape_Verde "Cape Verde"), [Madeira](/wiki/Madeira_Islands "Madeira Islands"), and the [Savage Isles](/wiki/Savage_Isles "Savage Isles").[[84]](#cite_note-Carracedo_Troll_North-East_Atlantic_Islands-85) The Canary Islands constitute the largest and most populated archipelago of the Macaronesia region.[[12]](#cite_note-catalogo.museosdetenerife.org-12) The archipelago has seven large and several smaller islands, all of which are volcanic in origin.[[85]](#cite_note-86)

According to the position of the islands with respect to the north-east [trade winds](/wiki/Trade_winds "Trade winds"), the climate can be mild and wet or very dry. Several native species form [laurisilva](/wiki/Laurisilva "Laurisilva") forests.

The individual islands in the Canary archipelago tend to have distinct [microclimates](/wiki/Microclimate "Microclimate"). Those islands such as [El Hierro](/wiki/El_Hierro "El Hierro"), [La Palma](/wiki/La_Palma "La Palma") and [La Gomera](/wiki/La_Gomera "La Gomera") lying to the west of the archipelago have a climate which is influenced by the moist [Canary Current](/wiki/Canary_Current "Canary Current"). They are well vegetated even at low levels and have extensive tracts of sub-tropical laurisilva forest. Travelling east toward the African coast, the influence of the current diminishes, and the islands become increasingly arid. [Fuerteventura](/wiki/Fuerteventura "Fuerteventura") and [Lanzarote](/wiki/Lanzarote "Lanzarote"), the islands which are closest to the African mainland, are effectively desert or semi-desert.

[Gran Canaria](/wiki/Gran_Canaria "Gran Canaria") is known as a "continent in miniature" for its diverse landscapes like [Maspalomas](/wiki/Maspalomas "Maspalomas") and [Roque Nublo](/wiki/Roque_Nublo "Roque Nublo"). The north of [Tenerife](/wiki/Tenerife "Tenerife") lies under the influence of the moist Atlantic winds and is well vegetated. The south of the island around the tourist resorts of [Playa de las Américas](/wiki/Playa_de_las_Am%C3%A9ricas "Playa de las Américas") and [Los Cristianos](/wiki/Los_Cristianos "Los Cristianos") is arid. The island rises to almost 4,000 m (13,000 ft) above sea level. At altitude, in the cool relatively wet climate, forests of the endemic pine *[Pinus canariensis](/wiki/Pinus_canariensis "Pinus canariensis")* thrive. Many of the plant species in the Canary Islands, like the Canary Island pine and the [dragon tree](/wiki/Dracaena_(plant) "Dracaena (plant)"), *[Dracaena draco](/wiki/Dracaena_draco "Dracaena draco")* are endemic, as noted by [Sabin Berthelot](/wiki/Sabin_Berthelot "Sabin Berthelot") and [Philip Barker Webb](/wiki/Philip_Barker_Webb "Philip Barker Webb") in their work, *L'Histoire Naturelle des Îles Canaries* (1835–50).[[86]](#cite_note-87)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canary_Islands&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Climate")]

The climate is warm [subtropical](/wiki/Subtropical_climate "Subtropical climate")/[tropical](/wiki/Tropical_climate "Tropical climate") and generally [arid](/wiki/Aridity "Aridity"), moderated by the sea and in summer by the [trade winds](/wiki/Trade_winds "Trade winds").[[87]](#cite_note-88) There are a number of [microclimates](/wiki/Microclimate "Microclimate") and the classifications range mainly from [semi-desert](/wiki/Hot_semi-arid_climate "Hot semi-arid climate") to [desert](/wiki/Hot_desert_climate "Hot desert climate"). The majority of the Canary Islands have a [hot desert climate](/wiki/Desert_climate "Desert climate") (*BWh*) and a [hot semi-desert climate](/wiki/Semi-arid_climate "Semi-arid climate") (*BSh*) within the [Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") system, caused partly due to the cool [Canary Current](/wiki/Canary_Current "Canary Current").[[88]](#cite_note-89) A subtropical humid climate, which is very influenced by the ocean, is in the middle of the islands of [La Gomera](/wiki/La_Gomera "La Gomera"), [Tenerife](/wiki/Tenerife "Tenerife") and [La Palma](/wiki/La_Palma "La Palma"), where [laurisilva](/wiki/Laurisilva "Laurisilva") cloud forests grow.[[89]](#cite_note-90)

### Geology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canary_Islands&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Geology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Parque_nacional_de_Garajonay_-_bosque_de_laurel.jpg/250px-Parque_nacional_de_Garajonay_-_bosque_de_laurel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Parque_nacional_de_Garajonay_-_bosque_de_laurel.jpg)

A humid [laurel forest](/wiki/Laurel_forest "Laurel forest") in [La Gomera](/wiki/La_Gomera "La Gomera")

Main article: [Geology of the Canary Islands](/wiki/Geology_of_the_Canary_Islands "Geology of the Canary Islands")

The seven major islands, one minor island, and several small islets were originally volcanic islands, formed by the [Canary hotspot](/wiki/Canary_hotspot "Canary hotspot").[[95]](#cite_note-96) The Canary Islands is the only place in Spain where [volcanic eruptions](/wiki/Volcanic_eruption "Volcanic eruption") have been recorded during the [Modern Era](/wiki/Modern_Era "Modern Era"), with some volcanoes still active (El Hierro, 2011).[[96]](#cite_note-97)
Volcanic islands such as those in the Canary chain often have steep ocean cliffs caused by catastrophic debris avalanches and [landslides](/wiki/Landslide "Landslide").[[97]](#cite_note-98) The island chain's most recent eruption occurred at [Cumbre Vieja](/wiki/Cumbre_Vieja "Cumbre Vieja"), a volcanic ridge on La Palma, [in 2021](/wiki/2021_Cumbre_Vieja_volcanic_eruption "2021 Cumbre Vieja volcanic eruption").[[98]](#cite_note-99)

The [Teide](/wiki/Teide "Teide") [volcano](/wiki/Volcano "Volcano") on Tenerife is the highest mountain in Spain, and the third tallest volcano on Earth on a volcanic ocean island.[[99]](#cite_note-100) All the islands except La Gomera have been active in the last million years. Four of them, Lanzarote, Tenerife, La Palma and El Hierro, have historical records of eruptions since European discovery.[[84]](#cite_note-Carracedo_Troll_North-East_Atlantic_Islands-85) The islands rise from [Jurassic](/wiki/Jurassic "Jurassic") [oceanic crust](/wiki/Oceanic_crust "Oceanic crust") associated with the opening of the Atlantic. Underwater [magmatism](/wiki/Magmatism "Magmatism") began during the [Cretaceous](/wiki/Cretaceous "Cretaceous"), and has continued to the present day. The islands were once considered as a distinct physiographic section of the [Atlas Mountains](/wiki/Atlas_Mountains "Atlas Mountains") province, which is part of the larger African Alpine System division, but are now recognized as being related to a magmatic [hot spot](/wiki/Hotspot_(geology) "Hotspot (geology)").[[84]](#cite_note-Carracedo_Troll_North-East_Atlantic_Islands-85)

In the summer of 2011, a series of low-magnitude earthquakes occurred beneath El Hierro. These had a linear trend of northeast–southwest. In October a submarine eruption occurred about 2 km (1+1⁄4 mi) south of Restinga. This eruption produced gases and pumice, but no explosive activity was reported.[[100]](#cite_note-101)

The following table shows the highest mountains in each of the islands:

### Natural symbols

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canary_Islands&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Natural symbols")]

Main article: [List of animal and plant symbols of the Canary Islands](/wiki/List_of_animal_and_plant_symbols_of_the_Canary_Islands "List of animal and plant symbols of the Canary Islands")

The official natural symbols associated with Canary Islands are the bird *[Serinus canaria](/wiki/Serinus_canaria "Serinus canaria")* (canary) and the *[Phoenix canariensis](/wiki/Phoenix_canariensis "Phoenix canariensis")* palm.[[105]](#cite_note-106)

### National parks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canary_Islands&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: National parks")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Caldera_de_Taburiente_La_Palma.jpg/250px-Caldera_de_Taburiente_La_Palma.jpg)](/wiki/File:Caldera_de_Taburiente_La_Palma.jpg)

The [Caldera de Taburiente National Park](/wiki/Caldera_de_Taburiente_National_Park "Caldera de Taburiente National Park") (La Palma)

Four of Spain's thirteen national parks are located in the Canary Islands, more than any other autonomous community. Two of these have been declared [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Sites "World Heritage Sites") and the other two are part of [Biosphere Reserves](/wiki/Biosphere_Reserves "Biosphere Reserves"). The parks are:[[106]](#cite_note-107)

| Park | Island | Area | Year of designation | UNESCO Status |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [Caldera de Taburiente National Park](/wiki/Caldera_de_Taburiente_National_Park "Caldera de Taburiente National Park") | La Palma | 46.9 km2 (18.1 sq mi) | 1954 | Part of the La Palma Biosphere Reserve since 2002 |
| [Garajonay National Park](/wiki/Garajonay_National_Park "Garajonay National Park") | La Gomera | 39.86 km2 (15.39 sq mi) | 1981 | World Heritage Site since 1986 |
| [Teide National Park](/wiki/Teide_National_Park "Teide National Park") | Tenerife | 189.9 km2 (73.3 sq mi) | 1954 | World Heritage Site since 2007 |
| [Timanfaya National Park](/wiki/Timanfaya_National_Park "Timanfaya National Park") | Lanzarote | 51.07 km2 (19.72 sq mi) | 1974 | Part of the Lanzarote Biosphere Reserve since 1993 |

[Teide National Park](/wiki/Teide_National_Park "Teide National Park") is the oldest and largest national park in the Canary Islands and one of the oldest in Spain. Located in the geographic centre of the island of Tenerife, it is the most visited national park in Spain.[[101]](#cite_note-diariodeavisos.com-102)[[102]](#cite_note-sanborondon.info-103) The park's highlight is the [Teide](/wiki/Teide "Teide") volcano. Standing at an altitude of 3,715 metres (12,188 ft),[[107]](#cite_note-IGN_PhysicalMap_2012-108) it is the highest elevation in Spain and the third largest volcano on Earth from its base. In 2007, the Teide National Park was declared one of the [12 Treasures of Spain](/wiki/12_Treasures_of_Spain "12 Treasures of Spain").

### Governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canary_Islands&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Governance")]

The regional executive body, the [Parliament of the Canary Islands](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_Canary_Islands "Parliament of the Canary Islands"), is presided over by [Fernando Clavijo Batlle](/wiki/Fernando_Clavijo_Batlle "Fernando Clavijo Batlle") ([Canarian Coalition](/wiki/Canarian_Coalition "Canarian Coalition")), the current [President of the Canary Islands](/wiki/President_of_the_Canary_Islands "President of the Canary Islands"). The members of the regional legislature, the [Parliament of the Canary Islands](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_Canary_Islands "Parliament of the Canary Islands"), has 70 elected legislators. The [last regional election](/wiki/2023_Canarian_regional_election "2023 Canarian regional election") took place in May 2023.

The islands have 14 seats in the [Spanish Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_Spain "Senate of Spain"). Of these, 11 seats are directly elected, 3 for Gran Canaria, 3 for Tenerife, and 1 each for Lanzarote (including La Graciosa), Fuerteventura, La Palma, La Gomera and El Hierro. The other 3 are appointed by the regional legislature.[[108]](#cite_note-compsenado-109)

### Political geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canary_Islands&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Political geography")]

The **Autonomous Community of the Canary Islands** has two [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Spain "Provinces of Spain") (*provincias*), [Las Palmas](/wiki/Province_of_Las_Palmas "Province of Las Palmas") and [Santa Cruz de Tenerife](/wiki/Province_of_Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife "Province of Santa Cruz de Tenerife"), whose capitals, [Las Palmas de Gran Canaria](/wiki/Las_Palmas_de_Gran_Canaria "Las Palmas de Gran Canaria") and [Santa Cruz de Tenerife](/wiki/Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife "Santa Cruz de Tenerife"), are capitals of the autonomous community. Each of the seven major islands are ruled by an island council named a *Cabildo Insular*. Each island is subdivided into smaller [municipalities](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Spain "Municipalities of Spain") (*municipios*). [Las Palmas is divided into 34 municipalities](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Las_Palmas "List of municipalities in Las Palmas"), and [Santa Cruz de Tenerife is divided into 54 municipalities](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife "List of municipalities in Santa Cruz de Tenerife").[[109]](#cite_note-110)

The international boundary of the Canaries is disputed in [Morocco–Spain relations](/wiki/Morocco%E2%80%93Spain_relations "Morocco–Spain relations"). In 2022 the UN declared the Canary Island's territorial waters as being Moroccan coast and Morocco has authorised [gas and oil exploration](/wiki/Hydrocarbon_exploration "Hydrocarbon exploration") in what the Canary Islands states to be Canarian territorial waters and [Western Sahara](/wiki/Spanish_Sahara "Spanish Sahara") waters.[[110]](#cite_note-111) Morocco's official position is that international laws regarding territorial limits do not authorise Spain to claim seabed boundaries based on the territory of the Canaries, since the Canary Islands enjoy a large degree of autonomy. In fact, the islands do not enjoy any special degree of autonomy, as each one of the Spanish regions is considered an [autonomous community](/wiki/Autonomous_community "Autonomous community"), with equal status to the European ones. Under the [Law of the Sea](/wiki/Law_of_the_Sea "Law of the Sea"), the only islands not granted territorial waters or an [exclusive economic zone](/wiki/Exclusive_economic_zone "Exclusive economic zone") (EEZ) are those that are not fit for human habitation, or do not have an economic life of their own, which is not the case of the Canary Islands.[[111]](#cite_note-112)

### Canarian nationalism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canary_Islands&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Canarian nationalism")]

Main article: [Canarian nationalism](/wiki/Canarian_nationalism "Canarian nationalism")

There are some pro-independence political parties, like the [National Congress of the Canaries](/wiki/National_Congress_of_the_Canaries "National Congress of the Canaries") (CNC) and the [Popular Front of the Canary Islands](/wiki/Popular_Front_of_the_Canary_Islands "Popular Front of the Canary Islands"). Their popular support is almost insignificant, with no presence in either the autonomous parliament or the *cabildos insulares*.[[112]](#cite_note-113) In a 2012 study by the [Centro de Investigaciones Sociológicas](/wiki/Centro_de_Investigaciones_Sociol%C3%B3gicas "Centro de Investigaciones Sociológicas"), when asked about national identity, the majority of respondents from the Canary Islands (53.8%) considered themselves Spanish and Canarian in equal measures, followed by 24% who consider themselves more Canarian than Spanish. 6.1% of the respondents considered themselves only Canarian, and 7% considered themselves only Spanish.[[113]](#cite_note-cis2956-114)

### Defense

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canary_Islands&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Defense")]

Main article: [Military of the Canary Islands](/wiki/Military_of_the_Canary_Islands "Military of the Canary Islands")

The defense of the territory is the responsibility of the [Spanish Armed Forces](/wiki/Spanish_Armed_Forces "Spanish Armed Forces"). Components of the [Army](/wiki/Spanish_Army "Spanish Army"), [Navy](/wiki/Spanish_Navy "Spanish Navy"), [Air Force](/wiki/Spanish_Air_and_Space_Force "Spanish Air and Space Force") and the [Civil Guard](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_Guard "Spanish Civil Guard") are based in the territory.

After the establishment of a democratic [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy") in Spain, [autonomy](/wiki/Self-governance "Self-governance") was granted to the Canaries via a law passed in 1982, with a newly established autonomous devolved government and parliament. In 1983, the first autonomous elections were held. The [Spanish Socialist Workers' Party](/wiki/Spanish_Socialist_Workers%27_Party "Spanish Socialist Workers' Party") (PSOE) won.[[114]](#cite_note-115)

### Capitals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canary_Islands&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Capitals")]

At present, the Canary Islands is the only autonomous community in Spain that has two capitals: [Santa Cruz de Tenerife](/wiki/Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife "Santa Cruz de Tenerife") and [Las Palmas de Gran Canaria](/wiki/Las_Palmas_de_Gran_Canaria "Las Palmas de Gran Canaria"), since the [Statute of Autonomy of the Canary Islands](/w/index.php?title=Statute_of_Autonomy_of_the_Canary_Islands&action=edit&redlink=1 "Statute of Autonomy of the Canary Islands (page does not exist)") [[es](https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Estatuto_de_Autonom%C3%ADa_de_Canarias "es:Estatuto de Autonomía de Canarias")] was created in 1982.[[21]](#cite_note-es.wikisource.org-21)[[22]](#cite_note-gobiernodecanarias.org-22)

The political capital of the archipelago did not exist as such until the nineteenth century. The first cities founded by the [Europeans](/wiki/Europeans "Europeans") at the time of the conquest of the Canary Islands in the 15th century were: [Telde](/wiki/Telde "Telde") (in [Gran Canaria](/wiki/Gran_Canaria "Gran Canaria")), [San Marcial del Rubicón](/w/index.php?title=San_Marcial_del_Rubic%C3%B3n&action=edit&redlink=1 "San Marcial del Rubicón (page does not exist)") (in [Lanzarote](/wiki/Lanzarote "Lanzarote")) and [Betancuria](/wiki/Betancuria "Betancuria") (in [Fuerteventura](/wiki/Fuerteventura "Fuerteventura")). These cities boasted the first European institutions present in the archipelago, including [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") [bishoprics](/wiki/Bishoprics "Bishoprics").[[115]](#cite_note-:1-116) Although, because the period of splendor of these cities developed before the total conquest of the archipelago and its incorporation into the [Crown of Castile](/wiki/Crown_of_Castile "Crown of Castile") never had a political and real control of the entire Canary archipelago.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Las_Palmas_panorama.jpg/330px-Las_Palmas_panorama.jpg)](/wiki/File:Las_Palmas_panorama.jpg)

Overview of Las Palmas de Gran Canaria

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Vista_de_Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife.png/330px-Vista_de_Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife.png)](/wiki/File:Vista_de_Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife.png)

View of Santa Cruz de Tenerife

The function of a Canarian city with full jurisdiction for the entire archipelago only exists after the conquest of the Canary Islands, although originally *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")*, that is, without legal and real meaning and linked to the headquarters of the [Canary Islands General Captaincy](/w/index.php?title=Canary_Islands_General_Captaincy&action=edit&redlink=1 "Canary Islands General Captaincy (page does not exist)").

Las Palmas de Gran Canaria was the first city that exercised this function. This is because the residence of the Captain General of the Canary Islands was in this city during part of the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries.[[116]](#cite_note-junta-117) In May 1661, the Captain General of the Canary Islands, [Jerónimo de Benavente y Quiñones](/w/index.php?title=Jer%C3%B3nimo_de_Benavente_y_Qui%C3%B1ones&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jerónimo de Benavente y Quiñones (page does not exist)"), moved the headquarters of the captaincy to the city of [San Cristóbal de La Laguna](/wiki/San_Crist%C3%B3bal_de_La_Laguna "San Cristóbal de La Laguna") on the island of [Tenerife](/wiki/Tenerife "Tenerife").[[117]](#cite_note-portalcultura.mde_1-118) This was due to the fact that this island since the conquest was the most populated, productive and with the highest economic expectations.[[118]](#cite_note-mdc.ulpgc_1-119) La Laguna would be considered the *de facto* capital of the archipelago[[119]](#cite_note-ref_duplicada_2-120) until the official status of the capital of Canary Islands in the city of Santa Cruz de Tenerife was confirmed in the 19th century, due in part to the constant controversies and rivalries between the bourgeoisies of San Cristóbal de La Laguna and Las Palmas de Gran Canaria for the economic, political and institutional hegemony of the archipelago.[[120]](#cite_note-121)

Already in 1723, the Captain General of the Canary Islands [Lorenzo Fernandez de Villavicencio](/w/index.php?title=Lorenzo_Fernandez_de_Villavicencio&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lorenzo Fernandez de Villavicencio (page does not exist)") had moved the headquarters of the General Captaincy of the Canary Islands from San Cristóbal de La Laguna to Santa Cruz de Tenerife. This decision continued without pleasing the society of the island of Gran Canaria.[[121]](#cite_note-122) It would be after the creation of the [Province of Canary Islands](/wiki/Province_of_Canary_Islands "Province of Canary Islands") in November 1833 in which Santa Cruz would become the first fully official capital of the Canary Islands (*[De jure](/wiki/De_jure "De jure")* and not of *de facto* as happened previously).[[21]](#cite_note-es.wikisource.org-21)[[24]](#cite_note-Real_Decreto_Canarias-24) Santa Cruz de Tenerife would be the capital of the Canary archipelago until during the Government of General [Primo de Rivera](/wiki/Miguel_Primo_de_Rivera "Miguel Primo de Rivera") in 1927 the province of Canary Islands was split in two provinces: [Las Palmas](/wiki/Province_of_Las_Palmas "Province of Las Palmas") with capital in Las Palmas de Gran Canaria, and [Santa Cruz de Tenerife](/wiki/Province_of_Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife "Province of Santa Cruz de Tenerife") with capital in the homonymous city.

Finally, with the Statute of Autonomy of the Canary Islands in 1982 and the creation of the Autonomous Community of the Canary Islands, the capital of the archipelago between Las Palmas de Gran Canaria and Santa Cruz de Tenerife is fixed, which is how it remains today.

As of 2024, the Canary Islands have a population of 2,238,754, making them the 7th-most populous of Spain's [autonomous communities](/wiki/Autonomous_communities "Autonomous communities").[[124]](#cite_note-population-125) The total area of the archipelago is 7,445.58 km2 (2,874.75 sq mi), resulting in a population density of 303.9 inhabitants per square kilometre.[[124]](#cite_note-population-125)[[3]](#cite_note-area-3)

The population of the islands as of 2024 is:[[124]](#cite_note-population-125)

* [Tenerife](/wiki/Tenerife "Tenerife") – 959,189
* [Gran Canaria](/wiki/Gran_Canaria "Gran Canaria") – 869,984
* [Lanzarote](/wiki/Lanzarote "Lanzarote") – 163,230 (including the population of [La Graciosa](/wiki/La_Graciosa "La Graciosa"))
* [Fuerteventura](/wiki/Fuerteventura "Fuerteventura") – 126,676
* [La Palma](/wiki/La_Palma "La Palma") – 85,382
* [La Gomera](/wiki/La_Gomera "La Gomera") – 22,507
* [El Hierro](/wiki/El_Hierro "El Hierro") – 11,786

The Canary Islands have become home to many European residents, mainly coming from Italy, Germany and the UK. Because of the vast immigration to Venezuela and Cuba during the second half of the 20th century and the later return to the Canary Islands of these people along with their families, there are many residents whose country of origin was [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela "Venezuela") (66,593) or [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba") (41,807). Since the 1990s, many [illegal migrants](/wiki/Immigration_to_Spain "Immigration to Spain") have reached the Canary Islands, [Melilla](/wiki/Melilla "Melilla") and [Ceuta](/wiki/Ceuta "Ceuta"), using them as entry points to the EU.[[125]](#cite_note-canarias7.es-126)[[126]](#cite_note-ine_pop_19_birthplace-127)[[127]](#cite_note-CIA-128) A record number of 46,843 migrants, mostly from [Senegal](/wiki/Senegal "Senegal"), [Mali](/wiki/Mali "Mali") and [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco"), arrived illegally in the Canary Islands in 2024, up from 39,910 in 2023.[[128]](#cite_note-129)
